---
title: "Introduction"
format: live-html
reference-location: margin
---

## Model Intuition

The core of the Solow model relies on the Cobb-Douglas production function with constant returns to scale.^[We can work with decreasing returns. Increasing returns are hard.] We represent total output $Y$ as a function of capital $K$, labor $L$, and the level of technology $A$:

$$
Y = F(K, AL) = K^\alpha (AL)^{1-\alpha}
$$

In per-capita terms, where $y = Y/AL$ and $k = K/AL$, the production function simplifies to:

::: {#nte-production .callout-note title="Production"}
Production of GDP per capita is determined as follows:
$$
y = f(k) = k^\alpha
$$

:::

[This is a span that has the class `column-margin` which places it in the margin without the sidenote mark.]{.column-margin}

## Simulating the Production Function

Below is a simple Python snippet to visualize how output $y$ responds to changes in capital intensity $k$ for different values of the capital share $\alpha$. It uses the production function in @nte-production

::: {#cell-fig-production-function .cell execution_count=1}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Define the production function
def production_function(k, alpha):
    return k**alpha

# Parameters
k_values = np.linspace(0, 5, 100)
alphas = [0.3, 0.5, 0.7]

# Plotting
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
for a in alphas:
    plt.plot(k_values, production_function(k_values, a), label=rf"$\alpha = {a}$")

plt.title("Diminishing Marginal Returns to Capital")
plt.xlabel("Capital per effective worker ($k$)")
plt.ylabel("Output per effective worker ($y$)")
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.show()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![Output per effective worker as a function of capital intensity.](testing_files/figure-html/fig-production-function-output-1.png){#fig-production-function width=665 height=451}
:::
:::
